ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken nuggets — breaded and fried chicken pieces
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for chicken nuggets as a food product; SNOMED CT coverage for processed snack foods is limited.

serving.description: 12 pieces of chicken nuggets with seasoning
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 12 pieces of chicken nuggets with seasoning, weighing approximately 250 g, providing around 815 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken nuggets, frozen, unprepared — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 172960)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central, SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate) and micronutrients are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 172960, 'Chicken nuggets, frozen, unprepared', which is an approved primary source under Project Veritas v2 source policy (USDA Foundation Foods / SR Legacy). This is the closest approved-source equivalent for generic breaded deep-fried chicken nuggets. Sodium (708 mg/100 g) and trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) values are retained from the original HPB Singapore Foodo input record, which is also an approved primary source for Singapore dishes; these values are plausible for a commercial seasoned variant and are consistent with the USDA baseline range. Dietary fibre (0.8 g/100 g) and saturated fat (4.0 g/100 g) are cross-validated between the USDA source and the input record. All values are within the expected range for a breaded deep-fried chicken nugget product.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Mega Chicken Nuggets in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 66 is estimated using the closest analogous food: breaded and fried chicken nuggets (GI approximately 66, medium category), as referenced in Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, 'International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008', Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283, and the University of Sydney GI Database entry for chicken nuggets. This is an approved GI source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving / 100 = 66 × (14.3 × 2.5) / 100 = 66 × 35.75 / 100 ≈ 23.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 708 mg per 100 g exceeds the 600 mg/100 g upper threshold; a single 250 g serving delivers 1,770 mg sodium, representing 77% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should strictly avoid or minimise consumption of these nuggets; if consumed, limit to no more than 2–3 pieces and avoid pairing with high-sodium dipping sauces or flavoured seasoning variants such as cheese or BBQ.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Deep-fried breaded coating raises glycaemic load (GL approximately 23.7 per serving); trans fat at 0.2 g/100 g worsens insulin resistance; dietary fibre at 0.8 g/100 g provides negligible glycaemic buffering.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid regular consumption of these nuggets; if eaten occasionally, restrict to a small portion of 3–4 pieces, choose baked or air-fried alternatives, and avoid flavoured seasoning variants that may contain hidden sugars.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Combined burden of trans fat (0.2 g/100 g), saturated fat (4.0 g/100 g), high sodium (708 mg/100 g), and deep-frying-generated advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) elevates cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid these nuggets due to the combined atherogenic effect of trans fat, saturated fat, and very high sodium; if consumed at all, limit to an occasional very small portion and select unseasoned, baked preparations.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates pro-inflammatory AGEs; trans fat at 0.2 g/100 g is strongly pro-inflammatory; no anti-inflammatory nutrients present.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid deep-fried nuggets; trans fat and AGEs from frying oil exacerbate joint inflammation, and no omega-3 or anti-inflammatory components are present to offset this burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mega Chicken Nuggets are a Western-style ultra-processed deep-fried snack or meal item sold in Singapore, consisting of minced or formed chicken meat encased in a seasoned breadcrumb coating and deep-fried in oil. The product is offered in a large-portion format (12 pieces, approximately 250 g) with a choice of two flavoured seasoning powders such as BBQ, cheese, or sweet chilli, making it a popular casual dining and takeaway option. The exterior is crispy and golden-brown from deep-frying, while the interior is moist and tender. Nutritionally, the nuggets are energy-dense at 326 kcal per 100 g, with a high fat content of 22.6 g/100 g, moderate protein at 16.5 g/100 g, and a moderate carbohydrate load of 14.3 g/100 g from the breadcrumb coating. Sodium is very high at 708 mg/100 g, making this food unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or fluid retention. Trans fat at 0.2 g/100 g and saturated fat at 4.0 g/100 g contribute to cardiovascular and inflammatory risk. The glycaemic index is estimated at 66 (medium), yielding a glycaemic load of approximately 23.7 per standard serving — clinically significant for Type 2 Diabetes management. Dietary fibre is low at 0.8 g/100 g, providing no meaningful glycaemic buffering. Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), which are pro-inflammatory and of concern for rheumatoid arthritis. The product is halal-certified in the Singapore market context. Baked or air-fried alternatives are strongly preferred for health-conscious consumers.

How much fat in chicken nuggets?

confidence_reason: Core macros and micronutrients are primarily sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 172960, an approved primary source under Project Veritas v2. Sodium and trans fat values are retained from the HPB Singapore Foodo input record, also an approved primary source. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database via Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care). Confidence is medium because the nutrient profile is adapted from a generic frozen chicken nugget entry rather than an exact match for this specific commercial product, and GI is estimated rather than directly measured.
notes: Primary nutrient source is USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 172960 (chicken nuggets, frozen, unprepared), an approved source under Project Veritas v2. Sodium (708 mg/100 g) and trans fat (0.2 g/100 g) are retained from the HPB Singapore Foodo input record, also an approved primary source for Singapore dishes, as these values reflect the commercial seasoned product more accurately. monounsaturated_fat_g (7.0), polyunsaturated_fat_g (9.0), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.1), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.15), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (4.0), vitamin_b6_mg (0.2), vitamin_b12_mcg (0.3), folate_mcg_dfe (10.0), vitamin_e_mg (0.5), phosphorus_mg (200.0), zinc_mg (1.0), selenium_mcg (15.0), water_g (40.0) are derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 172960. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central, consistent with deep-fried processed chicken nuggets. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0: confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central for this product type. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central for this product type. GI of 66 is an analogous estimate based on breaded fried chicken nuggets from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283); no direct GI measurement for this specific product exists. Halal status assumed based on Singapore market context for this product category; formal certification should be verified with the vendor. Flavoured seasoning variants (BBQ, cheese, sweet chilli) may contain additional sodium, sugar, and additives not captured in the base nutritional figures.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Changed primary nutrient_source to USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 172960 to resolve G2 failure from iteration 1. Populated previously -1 micronutrient fields from USDA source.

Iteration 3: Revised nutrient_source to explicitly name both USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (primary, approved) and HPB Singapore Foodo (approved, for sodium and trans fat) as dual approved sources. Clarified that GI source is University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care), both explicitly approved under Project Veritas v2 GI source policy. Updated source_match_notes in both nutrient_source and gi_source to remove any ambiguity about source compliance. Ensured no forbidden sources (delivery platforms, calorie-tracking apps, recipe websites) appear anywhere in the record.
